content.text: Two agents solving different tasks may need completely different architectures — not because the tasks are about different topics, but because the

differs. An agent playing chess can plan several moves ahead because the board stays still while it thinks and every piece is visible. An agent driving a car cannot — the road changes in milliseconds and sensors only show the local area.
) classify environments along several orthogonal dimensions. Understanding where your agent's environment falls on each axis directly determines what techniques are available to it.

attrs.alt: Environment type axes: Fully/Partially Observable, Deterministic/Stochastic, Static/Dynamic, Episodic/Sequential
attrs.title: Environment Types Classification

content.text: Encoding the environment profile as a typed object makes the classification explicit and enables automated architectural recommendations:

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum


class Observability(Enum):
    FULL    = "fully_observable"     # agent sees complete world state
    PARTIAL = "partially_observable" # agent sees only part of the world


class Determinism(Enum):
    DETERMINISTIC = "deterministic"  # same action → same outcome
    STOCHASTIC    = "stochastic"     # outcomes are probabilistic


class Dynamics(Enum):
    STATIC     = "static"      # world does not change while agent thinks
    DYNAMIC    = "dynamic"     # world changes while agent deliberates
    SEMIDYNAMIC = "semidynamic" # world static but agent's score changes (e.g. quiz timer)


class Episodicity(Enum):
    EPISODIC   = "episodic"    # each task is independent
    SEQUENTIAL = "sequential"  # decisions affect future tasks


class AgentCount(Enum):
    SINGLE = "single"  # only one agent
    MULTI  = "multi"   # multiple agents, may cooperate or compete


@dataclass
class EnvironmentProfile:
    name: str
    observability: Observability
    determinism:   Determinism
    dynamics:      Dynamics
    episodicity:   Episodicity
    agent_count:   AgentCount = AgentCount.SINGLE

    def complexity_score(self) -> int:
        """Higher = harder for agents to handle."""
        score = 0
        if self.observability == Observability.PARTIAL:  score += 2
        if self.determinism   == Determinism.STOCHASTIC: score += 2
        if self.dynamics      == Dynamics.DYNAMIC:       score += 2
        if self.dynamics      == Dynamics.SEMIDYNAMIC:   score += 1
        if self.episodicity   == Episodicity.SEQUENTIAL: score += 1
        if self.agent_count   == AgentCount.MULTI:       score += 2
        return score

    def recommended_architecture(self) -> str:
        s = self.complexity_score()
        if s <= 1:  return "Simple reflex agent"
        if s <= 3:  return "Model-based or goal-based agent"
        if s <= 5:  return "Utility-based agent with planning"
        return "Learning agent or LLM-based agent with memory"


# Examples
chess = EnvironmentProfile(
    "Chess",
    Observability.FULL, Determinism.DETERMINISTIC,
    Dynamics.STATIC, Episodicity.SEQUENTIAL,
    AgentCount.MULTI,
)
taxi = EnvironmentProfile(
    "Taxi driving",
    Observability.PARTIAL, Determinism.STOCHASTIC,
    Dynamics.DYNAMIC, Episodicity.SEQUENTIAL,
    AgentCount.MULTI,
)
spam_filter = EnvironmentProfile(
    "Spam filter",
    Observability.FULL, Determinism.DETERMINISTIC,
    Dynamics.STATIC, Episodicity.EPISODIC,
)

for env in [chess, taxi, spam_filter]:
    print(f"{env.name}: complexity={env.complexity_score()}, "
          f"architecture={env.recommended_architecture()}")
# Chess:        complexity=3, architecture=Model-based or goal-based agent
# Taxi driving: complexity=9, architecture=Learning agent or LLM-based agent
# Spam filter:  complexity=0, architecture=Simple reflex agent
When the agent cannot see the complete world state, it must
— an internal estimate of what the true state might be. The belief state is updated with each new percept and decayed for parts of the world not recently observed:

Partial observability is the single biggest driver of architectural complexity. An agent with full observability can use a lookup table. An agent with partial observability needs memory, inference, or an LLM-scale world model.
In a stochastic environment, the same action in the same state can produce different outcomes. Agents must either
(assuming the worst transition). Q-learning and Monte Carlo planning are designed specifically for stochastic environments:
Dynamic environments change while the agent is still deliberating. The key consequence:
. An agent that takes 10 seconds to decide in a market that moves every 100ms is effectively blind to 99% of the information. Design patterns for dynamic environments:
— produce a valid answer immediately, refine with more time. Always have a "best answer so far" ready.
— impose a hard deadline; return best answer when budget expires.
— fast reflexes handle urgent events; slow planning handles long-horizon goals.

import time
from threading import Timer


class DynamicEnvironment:
    """Environment that changes independently while the agent runs."""

    def __init__(self):
        self.stock_price = 100.0
        self._running = False

    def tick(self) -> None:
        """Simulate price movement every second."""
        import random
        self.stock_price *= (1 + random.uniform(-0.02, 0.02))
        if self._running:
            t = Timer(1.0, self.tick)
            t.daemon = True
            t.start()

    def start(self) -> None:
        self._running = True
        self.tick()

    def stop(self) -> None:
        self._running = False


class TimeBoundedAgent:
    """Agent with a time budget per decision for dynamic environments."""

    def __init__(self, env: DynamicEnvironment, budget_ms: float = 100):
        self.env = env
        self.budget = budget_ms / 1000

    def decide(self) -> str:
        """Make best decision within the time budget."""
        start = time.time()
        best_action = "hold"
        price = self.env.stock_price

        # Evaluate options within budget
        while time.time() - start < self.budget:
            # Anytime algorithm: refine within time budget
            if price < 95:
                best_action = "buy"
            elif price > 110:
                best_action = "sell"
            break  # single-step here; real anytime algo would deepen

        return best_action


# env = DynamicEnvironment()
# env.start()
# agent = TimeBoundedAgent(env, budget_ms=50)
# print(agent.decide())  # decides within 50ms even as price changes
# env.stop()
environments, each task is independent — the agent's action today cannot help or hurt it tomorrow. Image classification is episodic: classifying one photo has no effect on the next. In
environments, the agent's current actions constrain future options — using fuel now means less fuel later:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field


@dataclass
class SequentialState:
    """State in a sequential environment where past actions matter."""
    position: int = 0
    fuel: int = 10
    visited: list[int] = field(default_factory=list)


class SequentialPlanningAgent:
    """
    Agent that reasons about the long-term consequences of actions.
    Moving costs fuel; revisiting a tile wastes moves.
    """

    def __init__(self, goal: int = 8):
        self.goal = goal
        self.state = SequentialState()

    def evaluate_action(self, action: str) -> float:
        """Lookahead: how good is this action for future outcomes?"""
        next_pos = self.state.position + (1 if action == "forward" else -1)
        fuel_after = self.state.fuel - 1

        if fuel_after <= 0:
            return -100.0  # will run out of fuel
        if next_pos in self.state.visited:
            return -5.0   # revisiting is wasteful
        progress = self.goal - next_pos
        return -progress - 0.5  # closer to goal is better; penalise distance

    def act(self) -> str:
        """Choose action with best long-term value."""
        forward_val  = self.evaluate_action("forward")
        backward_val = self.evaluate_action("backward")
        return "forward" if forward_val >= backward_val else "backward"

    def execute(self, action: str) -> None:
        self.state.visited.append(self.state.position)
        self.state.position += 1 if action == "forward" else -1
        self.state.fuel -= 1


agent = SequentialPlanningAgent(goal=8)
for step in range(10):
    if agent.state.position == agent.goal or agent.state.fuel == 0:
        break
    action = agent.act()
    agent.execute(action)

print(f"Final position: {agent.state.position}, fuel left: {agent.state.fuel}")
# Final position: 8, fuel left: 2
No internal state — reactive policy is sufficient
Belief state or memory (LSTM, context window, vector DB)
No real-time constraint — batch reasoning is fine
Anytime algorithms, time-bounded decisions, reactive layers
Stateless policy; no cross-episode memory required
Long-horizon planning; value function over future states
Game theory, cooperation protocols, adversarial planning
When multiple agents share the same environment, two additional concerns arise:
(two agents want the same resource) and
(agents need to divide labour or negotiate). Multi-agent environments can be cooperative, competitive, or mixed. The key architectural implication is that the agent must model
— agents optimise against each other (adversarial). Examples: chess, trading, auctions.
— agents share a common objective. Examples: rescue robots, distributed pipeline workers.
— cooperate within teams, compete across teams. Examples: team sports, market competition.
Because environment type drives design, test the classification logic and the belief/planning components separately:

import pytest


class TestEnvironmentProfile:
    def test_simple_env_recommends_reflex_agent(self):
        env = EnvironmentProfile(
            "Thermostat",
            Observability.FULL, Determinism.DETERMINISTIC,
            Dynamics.STATIC, Episodicity.EPISODIC,
        )
        assert env.complexity_score() == 0
        assert "reflex" in env.recommended_architecture().lower()

    def test_complex_env_recommends_learning_agent(self):
        env = EnvironmentProfile(
            "Autonomous drone",
            Observability.PARTIAL, Determinism.STOCHASTIC,
            Dynamics.DYNAMIC, Episodicity.SEQUENTIAL,
            AgentCount.MULTI,
        )
        assert env.complexity_score() >= 8
        assert "learning" in env.recommended_architecture().lower()

    def test_partial_observation_adds_to_score(self):
        base = EnvironmentProfile(
            "base", Observability.FULL, Determinism.DETERMINISTIC,
            Dynamics.STATIC, Episodicity.EPISODIC,
        )
        partial = EnvironmentProfile(
            "partial", Observability.PARTIAL, Determinism.DETERMINISTIC,
            Dynamics.STATIC, Episodicity.EPISODIC,
        )
        assert partial.complexity_score() > base.complexity_score()


class TestBeliefState:
    def test_update_sets_value_and_confidence(self):
        bs = BeliefState()
        bs.update("location", "room_A", 0.9)
        assert bs.get("location") == "room_A"
        assert bs.confidences["location"] == pytest.approx(0.9)

    def test_decay_reduces_all_confidences(self):
        bs = BeliefState()
        bs.update("loc", "A", 1.0)
        bs.update("bat", 80, 1.0)
        bs.decay(0.5)
        assert bs.confidences["loc"] == pytest.approx(0.5)
        assert bs.confidences["bat"] == pytest.approx(0.5)
against its specification. Never couple them — the environment may change (stochastic → deterministic in tests) without touching the agent code.
Environment types are classified across four key axes:

(episodic vs sequential). Each combination demands different agent capabilities: partial observability requires belief states, stochasticity requires probabilistic planning, dynamic environments require time-bounded decisions, and sequential environments require long-horizon planning. Mapping your specific agent's environment to these axes before choosing an architecture avoids the most common mismatches between technique and problem.
